pattern: Treating the array itself as a hash table by placing each value at its own index lets you find the smallest absent positive in linear time and constant extra space.
hints: The answer must lie between 1 and n+1, so only values in that window can ever matter.
If every value v in [1, n] could sit at position v-1, a single scan would reveal the first index whose occupant is wrong.
Swap each in-range positive into its target slot (nums[i] should hold i+1), ignoring duplicates and out-of-range values, then scan for the first index where nums[i] != i+1; if all match, return n+1.

edge_inputs.description: missing positive sits inside the array range
negatives and duplicates around a gap
all values above the range so answer is 1
single element equal to one means answer is two
single non-positive means answer is one
contiguous run forces the answer to n plus one
duplicates must not be miscounted as filling the gap
large positives mixed with the small missing one

stress.description: permutation of 1..n with random replacements so the array is dense with in-range values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = list(range(1, n + 1))
    rng.shuffle(nums)
    for _ in range(n // 5):
        i = rng.randrange(n)
        nums[i] = rng.randrange(-1000, 1000)
    return [nums]
stress.note: dense in-range values exercise the swap-into-place loop heavily
